Items.Title: 内联 vs 内联块 vs 块 CSS 属性 | Inline vs Inline-Block vs Block CSS properties
Items.Description:
在 Web 浏览器中显示元素是每个前端程序员必须了解的基础知识之一。

有 3 种类型的 CSS 显示属性来表征每个 HTML 元素： inline 、 block和inline-block属性。 那么选择哪一个，为什么？

本文解释了这三种方法之间的区别，并给出了每种方法的示例。

块元素，顾名思义，就是占据整个块，或者说占据所有空间的元素。

由于块元素占据了所有位置，因此它不接受旁边的任何其他元素。

举例胜于文字，让我们举一个简单段落的例子。


所以这是一个简单的段落，在浏览器中看起来像这样：


让我们看看块元素的行为。

为此，我们必须访问浏览器的开发工具。

因此，右键单击鼠标并转到“检查”区域，单击“检查”，单击后将打开浏览器控制台。

打开后转到“Elements”而不是“Console”，在那里你会看到所有的 HTML 代码。

另一种无需右键单击即可打开开发工具的方法是单击 ctrl + shift + i。


进入“元素”选项卡后，只需将鼠标悬停在您的段落上即可。 您将看到以下结果：


这一切意味着什么？

可以看到，在开发工具中将鼠标悬停在段落上之后，在浏览器中我们可以清楚地看到该段落在一个蓝色的空间或者矩形内，而这个蓝色的空间被橙色的空间包围着。

这个橙色空间是什么意思？

橙色空间表示边距。 它是本段和其他 HTML 元素之间设置的边距。 并且在开发工具中可以看到右边方框中橙色空格的说明。 只需转到右侧并向下滚动一点：


所以橙色空间表示“边距”，黄色“边界”，绿色“填充”。

正是这个橙色空间使元素成为块元素。 就是这个橙色的空间不让任何其他元素和段落在同一行，段落因为这个橙色的空间占据了所有的块，因为正如你看到的，橙色的空间占据了页面的所有宽度.

换个角度来看，我们可以说块元素是傲慢的，不让任何人接近它。 真是个难缠的家伙！ 元素是块元素，因为默认情况下 CSS 属性display设置为block 。

另一方面是内联元素。

与块元素不同，内联元素是适度的，并且接受其他 HTML 元素紧挨着它。 我们以一张图片为例：

所以在这里我拍了一张简单的图片，并使用以下 CSS 代码调整了它的大小，因为它太大了：

所以最后我有以下图片：

同样，我们将转到开发工具并打开“元素”选项卡，然后将鼠标悬停在图像 HTML 代码上。

所以在浏览器中我们将有：

您现在可能已经注意到，当我们将鼠标悬停在开发工具中的图像上时，在浏览器中，图像位于蓝色空间内，周围没有橙色空间。

这就是图像是内联元素的原因。 因为它只占用为其宽度量身定制的空间，而不是整个页面的宽度。

在没有橙色空间的情况下，HTML 元素可以接受其他 HTML 元素紧挨着它。

因此，如果我们想在猫图像旁边添加另一个图像，我们可以。 让我们添加另一张图片：

我也会调整第二张图片的大小。 所以我们会有：

在浏览器中，我们有：

如您所见，这两个图像现在并排放置。

但是如果你试图在图像旁边添加一个段落它是行不通的，因为正如我们所说，段落是一个块元素，而块元素是自大的。

所以它会生成一个返回到一个新行，然后段落将在新行中创建。

元素是内联元素，因为默认情况下 CSS 属性display设置为inline 。

现在假设我们希望 2 个图像和段落在一行中。 怎么做？

那么，CSS 属性inline-block来了。

让我们为段落赋予inline-block的属性。

在浏览器中，我们有：


所以现在我们已经对齐了 3 个元素。 但这在幕后是如何运作的呢？

让我们再去一次开发工具并打开它。

有趣的是，当您将鼠标悬停在该段落上时。 你会看到这个：

正如您所看到的，由于inline-block属性，导致段落傲慢的橙色空间现在减少了。 一旦它被缩减，它就允许之前是块元素的段落，将所有空间都占为己有，与其他 HTML 元素共享空间，并接受紧挨着它的元素。

但是为什么我们选择inline-block属性呢？ 为什么我们不直接将段落的display CSS 属性设置为inline而不是inline-block ？ 因为它也会起作用，而且所有元素也会对齐。

要回答这个问题，请阅读下一段。

让我们举一个长段落的例子。


所以在浏览器中看起来像这样：

现在让我们将显示属性设置为内联，并为段落添加一些左、右、上和下边距。


所以在浏览器中我们有：

当我们添加内联属性时，段落变成了内联元素。

如您所见，内联元素不考虑顶部和底部边距，在这种特殊情况下也不考虑左侧和右侧边距。

虽然第一行向左移动了一点点，但其他行仍然没有移动，更不用说第一行和页面顶部之间的空间了。 根本没有空间。

而如果我们将段落的显示属性设置为inline-block ，我们将得到以下结果：

这是预期的结果。 所有边距都得到尊重。

另一个区别是宽度和高度属性。

让我们删除margin属性并返回到我们的段落，该段落具有inline作为 CSS 属性。

所以现在如果我们想为我们的段落设置宽度和高度，我们将添加以下代码：


如果您刷新浏览器，您将看到没有任何变化。

但是，如果我们将段落的 display 属性设置为inline-block并添加宽度和高度：

在浏览器中，我们的段落将是：

所以我们可以得出结论，使用inline-block属性，我们可以设置宽度、高度和边距，它们都会得到尊重。

但是对于内联属性，即使我们设置了宽度和高度以及边距属性，它们也不会受到尊重。

这就是为什么当我们想要使所有元素对齐时，我们使用inline-block over inline属性，以保留 HTML 元素的属性，以防我们想要添加width或height或margin 。

因此，为了使 2 个图像和段落对齐，我们将所有元素的display属性设置为inline-block 。

总结起来，HTML 元素有 3 种类型的显示属性：

  • 排队
  • 堵塞
  • 因此，最佳做法是在代码中包含内联块元素。

    绝对和相对位置：有什么区别？
    列表样式类型 CSS
    CSS border: How to add a border and make it rounded? CSS 边框：如何添加边框并使其变圆？



原文:

CSS has become one of the most important aspects of web design. Displaying elements in the web browser is one of the basics that every front-end programmer must know.

There are 3 types of CSS display properties that characterize every HTML elements: inline, block and inline-block properties. So which one to choose and why?

This article explains the differences between these three methods and gives examples of each.

1)-Block element

A block element, as the name indicates, is an element that occupies all the block, or in other words, all the space.

As a block element takes all the place, it does not accept any other element next to it.

As an example is better than words, let’s take an example of a simple paragraph.


So this is a simple paragraph that looks in the browser like this:


Let’s see the behavior of a block element.

To do that we have to access the development tool of the browser.

So Right click on the mouse and go to the “Inspect” area, click on “Inspect” and once clicked the browser console will open.

Once opened go to “Elements” instead of “Console”, and there you will see all your HTML code.

Another way to open the development tool without right click is to click on ctrl + shift + i.


Once in the “Elements” tab just go and hover over your paragraph. You will see the following results:


What does all that mean?

As you can see, after hovering over the paragraph in the development tool, on the browser we can clearly see that the paragraph is inside a blue space or rectangle, and this blue space is surrounded by an orange space.

And what does this orange space mean?

The orange space means the margin. It is the margin set between this paragraph and other HTML elements. And you can see the explanation of the orange space in the box on the right in the development tool. Just go to the right and scroll a little bit down:


So the orange space means “margin”, the yellow “border”, and the green “padding”.

And it is this orange space that makes an element a block element. It is this orange space that does not let any other element to be in the same line as the paragraph, and the paragraph occupies all the block because of this orange space, because as you see, the orange space takes all the width of the page.

Other way to view things, we can say that a block element is arrogant and does not let anyone approach it. What a difficult guy ! 😤

An element is a block element because by default the CSS property display is set to block.

2)- Inline element

On the other hand there is the inline element.

Unlike block element, inline element is modest, and accepts other HTML elements to be next to it. 😀

Let’s take an image as an example:

So here I took a simple image and I resized it using the following CSS code because it is so big:

So at the end I have the following image:

Now same thing, we’ll go to the development tool and open the “Elements” tab, and we will hover over the image HTML code.

So in the browser we’ll have:

As you have probably noticed by now, when we hover over the image in the development tool, on the browser the image is inside a blue space, and there is no orange space that surrounds it.

That’s why the image is an inline element. Because it occupies only the space tailored to its width, and not the width of the entire page.

In the absence of the orange space, the HTML element can accept other HTML elements to be next to it.

So if we want for example to add another image next to the cat image we can. Let’s add another image:

I will resize the second image as well. So we’ll have:

In the browser we have:

As you can see, the two images are side by side now.

However if you try to add a paragraph next to the image it will not work, because as we said, the paragraph is a block element, and a block element is arrogant.

So it will generate a return to a new line, and then the paragraph will be created in the new line.

An element is an inline element because by default the CSS property display is set to inline.

3)- Inline-block element

Now let’s say that we want the 2 images and the paragraph to be in one line. How to do that?

Well, here comes the CSS property inline-block.

Let’s give the paragraph the property of inline-block.

The CSS part will be:


In the browser we have:


So now we have the 3 elements aligned. But how does this work behind the scenes?

Let’s go one more time to the development tool and open it.

What is interesting is when you hover over the paragraph. You will see this:

As you can see, the orange space that is responsible for the paragraph being arrogant is reduced now with the inline-block property. And once it is reduced it allows to the paragraph that was a block element before, taking all the space to itself, to share the space with other HTML elements and accept elements to be next to it.

But why we chose inline-block property? Why we did not just set the display CSS property of the paragraph to inline instead of inline-block? Because it would have worked also and all the elements would have been aligned as well.

To answer this question read the next paragraph.

4)- Difference between inline and inline-block

Let’s take an example of a long paragraph.


So in the browser that will look like this:

Now let’s set the display property to inline and add some left, right, top and bottom margins to the paragraph.


So in the browser we have:

When we added the inline property the paragraph became an inline element.

As you can see, an inline element does not respect the top and bottom margins, and in this particular case neither the left and right margins.

Although the first line moved a little bit to the left, but still the other lines did not move at all, not to mention the space between the first line and the top of page. There is no space at all.

whereas if we set the display property of the paragraph to inline-block we’ll have this result:

Which is the result expected. All the margins are respected.

Another difference is the width and the height properties.

Let’s remove the margin property and go back to our paragraph which has inline as CSS property.

So now if we want to set a width and a height to our paragraph we’ll add the following code:


If you refresh the browser you’ll see nothing has changed.

However, if we set the display property of the paragraph to inline-block and we add a width and a height:

In the browser our paragraph will be:

So what we can conclude is that with inline-block property we can set a width and a height and margins, and they all will be respected.

But with inline property, even if we set width and height and margin properties, they are not respected.

That’s why we use inline-block over inline property when we want to make all the elements aligned, to preserve the HTML element’s properties in case if we want to add width or height or margin.

So to make the the 2 images and the paragraph aligned, we will set display property of all the elements to inline-block.

5)- Conclusion

To sum up there are 3 types of display properties for an HTML element:

  • inline
  • block
  • inline-block inline property does not respect the width, height or margin if they’re added, whilst inline-block and block properties do.

So best practice is to have inline-block elements in your code.

Items.Title: 使用 Kaggle 的 SageMaker 数据摄取 | SageMaker Data Ingestion using Kaggle
Items.Description:
最近，我一直在专注于学习AI/ML。 在克服了许多障碍和错误之后，我现在可以自信地分享一个成功的解决方案。

我打算解释 ML 的一个特定基础，即数据摄取。 我将演示如何将 Kaggle 数据集导入 SageMaker Studio 笔记本。 如果集成得当，您可以清楚地看到这两种工具如何相互补充。

先决条件

  • 需要 SageMaker Studio Notebook。 如果您还没有，可以按照本指南创建一个。
  • 需要一个 Kaggle 账号，你可以在这里注册一个。

首先，我们将导入将在笔记本中使用的 python 包。

导入包

import pandas as pd import time

安装 Kaggle CLI

要使用 Kaggle API，您必须拥有一个帐户和一个 API 令牌。 您可以按照本指南生成您的 API 令牌，它是完全免费的。 下面的命令创建一个 json 文件来存储您的 Kaggle 凭据。 在代码块中插入您的用户名和 API 密钥。

 ! touch ~/.kaggle/kaggle.json # Creates json file to store Kaggle API Credentials kaggle_api_token = { "username" : "<username>" , "key" : "<api_key>" } # Insert your own username and API Key here

然后我们将我们的 kaggle 凭据写入我们创建的 json 文件。

 import json # Writes API Credentials to Kaggle file with open ( '/root/.kaggle/kaggle.json' , 'w' ) as file : json . dump ( kaggle_api_token , file )

出于安全原因，我们必须确保其他用户没有对我们的 Kaggle 凭证的读取权限。

 ! chmod 600 ~/.kaggle/kaggle.json

由于我们的访问令牌现在已配置，我们可以列出可用的数据集。

 ! kaggle datasets list # List available datasets

如果上述命令成功，您将看到可用数据集的列表。

以下命令下载您指定的数据集。 您可以将此名称更改为数据集列表中返回的任何名称。 下载数据集可能需要一些时间，具体取决于您的网络连接。

 %% time ! kaggle datasets download - d iamsouravbanerjee / game - of - thrones - dataset -- unzip # Downloads & Unzip dataset

现在数据集已下载，让我们想象一下 csv 文件的样子。 我们将使用 pandas 来加载和显示数据。

 data = pd . read_csv ( "Game_of_Thrones.csv" , header = 0 ) df = data . copy () df . head ()

您可以在我的GitHub 上找到完整的 SageMaker Studio Notebook。

附加的功能

这是数据摄取的简单演示，您可以通过使用 pandas 从数据中提取见解或训练 ML 模型来构建此解决方案。 如果你这样做，请随时与我分享你的项目。

保持好奇，不断学习，不断建设！！！



原文:

Recently, I have been focusing on learning AI/ML. After overcoming many roadblocks and mistakes, I can now confidently share a successful solution.

I intend to explain one specific foundation of ML which is data ingestion. I will demonstrate how you can import a Kaggle dataset into a SageMaker Studio notebook. Amazon SageMaker is a ML tool that enables developers to rapidly create, train, and deploy machine-learning models in the cloud. Kaggle is an online community platform that has numerous datasets and ML challenges for data scientists and machine learning enthusiasts. You can clearly see how both tools can complement each other if integrated properly.

Prerequisite

  • A SageMaker Studio Notebook is needed. If you don't have one already, you can follow this guide to create one.
  • A Kaggle account is needed, you can register for one here.

🛠 Let's build!!

First we will import the python packages that will be used in the notebook.

Import Packages 

import pandas as pd
import time

Install Kaggle CLI

To use Kaggle API, you must have an account and an API token. You can follow this guide to generate your API token, it is completely free. The command below creates a json file to store your Kaggle credentials. Insert your username and API Key in the code blocks.

!touch ~/.kaggle/kaggle.json # Creates json file to store Kaggle API Credentials
kaggle_api_token = {"username":"<username>","key":"<api_key>"} # Insert your own username and API Key here

We then write our kaggle credentials to the json file we created.

import json # Writes API Credentials to Kaggle file
with open('/root/.kaggle/kaggle.json', 'w') as file: json.dump(kaggle_api_token,file)

For security reasons, we must ensure that other users do not have read access to our Kaggle credentials.

!chmod 600 ~/.kaggle/kaggle.json

Since our access token is now configured we can list the available datasets.

!kaggle datasets list # List available datasets

If the above command was successful, you will see a list of available datasets.

The below command downloads the dataset you specified. You can change this name to any of the names returned in the list of datasets. Downloading the dataset might take some time depending on your network connection.

%%time !kaggle datasets download -d iamsouravbanerjee/game-of-thrones-dataset --unzip # Downloads & Unzip dataset

Now that the dataset is downloaded, let us visualize what the csv file looks like. We will use pandas to load and display the data.

data = pd.read_csv("Game_of_Thrones.csv", header=0)
df = data.copy()
df.head()

Github Repo

You can find the complete SageMaker Studio Notebook on my GitHub.

Additional Features

This was a simple demonstration of data ingestion, you can build on this solution by extracting insights from the data using pandas or perhaps training an ML model. If you do, please feel free to share your project with me.

Stay curious, keep learning and keep building!!!

Items.Title: 超级应用程序如何改变金融科技格局 | How Super Apps Are Changing the FinTech Landscape
Items.Description:
超级应用程序是一个生态系统，是共享集成 UI 或用户界面的互连应用程序的集合。 该生态系统包含在一个大型应用程序中。 此外，一些人会将超级应用程序定义为包括数字钱包或忠诚度奖励系统，作为提供“粘性”的一种方式，或者用户将继续停留并使用该产品的机会。 忠诚度积分或奖励是产生粘性的创造性解决方案，因为它鼓励用户参与平台并从系统中获得积极的强化。

但超级应用程序在几个基本方面不同于普通应用程序。

普通应用程序需要定期更新和下载。 用户需要几个不同的应用程序来满足他们的需求。 超级应用程序不需要定期同步更新或不断下载，因为它们包含的“迷你应用程序”会自动更新。 与十几个常规应用程序相比，运行单个超级应用程序所需的内存和电量更少。

超级应用程序还提供了更加无缝的用户旅程。 当一个超级应用程序将多个不同的相关服务捆绑在一起时，用户无需跳转到另一个应用程序或被重定向到单独的窗口即可访问这些服务。 不仅了解用户喜欢什么，而且了解他们希望如何到达那里，这对于创建吸引用户并推动参与的引人入胜的产品非常重要。

为什么超级应用对金融科技很重要

超级应用可以是任何东西，无论它们服务于更多的受众还是更少的受众。 但超级应用程序已经扩展到金融领域，作为对市场的颠覆。 超级应用在本质上倾向于金融科技，并成为许多金融科技公司的重要核心产品。

但为什么这对开发人员很重要？ 使用 API 和集成第三方白标服务可以更轻松地构建平台。 过去，超级应用的方方面面都必须从头开始构建。 过去，银行业和更传统的金融机构被视为金融服务的“一站式商店”。 随着 2010 年代初消费者数字钱包的诞生，数字金融服务已经扩展到网上银行之外。 数字钱包简化了消费者的购买，提供了一种简化的在线消费和收款方式。 对于希望在线开展业务的人们来说，这是一个巨大的推动力。

金融科技领域的超级应用程序已经开始成为满足消费者金融需求的一站式商店。 这使他们与银行竞争，这导致多家银行与超级应用程序合作以获得最广泛的用户群。

更简单、成本更低的财务管理以换取优质服务是整个金融科技行业的重要目标。 当提供不同的选项时，可以更有效地满足用户的需求。

全球采用超级应用

这款超级应用的采用率最高的地区是亚洲和拉丁美洲。 中国是超级应用程序的最大采用者之一。 国内排名前三的超级应用拥有超过十亿的用户群。

由于不同的监管要求，超级应用最能满足亚洲普通用户的需求，因为对软件和发布应用程序的监管更为严格。 这为已经建立的应用程序提供了向现有核心用户群提供额外功能和服务的机会。

超级应用程序在西方的采用速度较慢。 各个应用程序之间的竞争更加激烈，并且公众愿意使用数十种独立的数字服务来满足他们的日常需求。

在美国，金融科技和金融领域的超级应用程序有所增加。 未来，超级应用程序可能会在西方找到一席之地，作为传统银行业务的更便宜的替代品，为个人提供更灵活和量身定制的体验。 随着数字钱包使用的增加，金融应用程序可能有机会扩大并为其用户群提供更多金融服务。

未来的趋势

未来超级应用程序的激增将会增加。 亚洲的超级应用正在经历最快的增长，并在一个地方提供数十种消费者服务。 在西方，超级应用程序可能会出现以填补利基市场或针对特定行业以提供无缝的用户体验。

大公司和品牌可能最有能力在美国创建超级应用程序，因为这些公司拥有庞大的用户群。 知名品牌可能最有可能构建成功的超级应用程序。

超级应用程序还可以作为生态系统，不仅为用户服务，也为开发者服务。 想象一下未来，开发人员可以制作始终与他人相互关联的项目。 金融科技可以实现这样的未来，一个更加互联的世界正在被创造出来。



原文:

A super app is an ecosystem, a collection of interconnected applications that share an integrated UI, or user interface. This ecosystem is contained within one large application. In addition, some would define super apps to include a digital wallet or loyalty rewards system as a way to provide “stickiness”, or the chance that a user will continue to stay and use the product. Loyalty points or rewards is a creative solution to produce stickiness because it encourages users to engage with the platform and receive positive reinforcement from the system.

But super apps differ from regular apps in several fundamental ways.

Normal apps require regular updates and downloads. The user needs several different apps to meet their needs. Super apps do not require regular simultaneous updates or constant downloads, because the “mini apps” they contain are updated automatically. Less memory and power is needed to run a single super app, compared to a dozen regular applications.

Super apps also provide a more seamless user journey. When a super app bundles several different related services together, the user gets to access these services without jumping to another app, or being redirected to a separate window. Knowing not only what users prefer, but how they would like to get there, is important for creating engaging products that captivate the user and drive engagement.

Why Super Apps Matter to Fintech

Super apps can be about anything, whether they serve a larger audience or a smaller audience. But super apps have been expanding into the world of finance, as a disruption to the marketplace. Super apps are inclined to be fintech in nature, and serve as an important core offering for many fintech companies.

But why does this matter to developers? Building a platform is getting easier with APIs and integrating third party white-label services. In the past, every aspect of the super app had to be built from the ground up. The Rapyd Developer Community is a great place to learn how to integrate Rapyd’s APIs with your platform.

Banking and more traditional financial institutions have been viewed as a “one stop shop” for financial services in the past. With the birth of the consumer digital wallet in the early 2010s, digital financial services have expanded beyond just online banking. The digital wallet simplified purchases for the consumer, providing a simplified way to spend and receive money online. This was a huge boost for people looking to conduct business online.

Super apps within the fintech space have started to become a one-stop shop for the consumer’s financial needs. This places them in competition with banks, which has resulted with several banks partnering with super apps to gain access to the widest user base.

Financial management that is easier and costs less in return for quality services is an important goal for the fintech industry as a whole. When different options are provided, the needs of the user can be met more efficiently.

Global Adoption of Super Apps

The highest rate of adoption for the super app has been in Asia, and Latin America. China is one of the largest adopters of super apps. The top three super apps within the country boast a user base of over a billion people.

Due to different regulatory requirements, super apps best meet the needs of the average user in Asia since there are stricter regulations surrounding software and publishing applications. This provided the opportunity for already established apps to provide additional features and services to an existing core user base.

The adoption of the super app within the West has been slower. There is more competition between individual apps, and a willingness from the public to engage with dozens of independent digital services to meet their daily needs.

Within the United States, there has been an increase in super apps within the fintech and financial space. In the future, the super app may find a place in the West as a cheaper alternative to traditional banking that offers a more flexible and tailored experience for the individual. With the increased use of digital wallets, financial apps may have the opportunity to expand and offer more financial services to their user base.

Future Trends

The proliferation of super apps will increase in the future. Superapps in Asia are experiencing the fastest growth, and provide dozens of consumer services in one place. Within the West, super apps may emerge to fill niche markets or target specific industries to provide a seamless user experience.

Large companies and brands may be the best positioned to create superapps in the US because the user base that these companies have is so large. Brands that are recognizable may be the most likely to build successful super apps.

Super apps can also serve as ecosystems not only for the user, but for the developers as well. Imagine a future when developers can make projects that are always interconnected with others. Such a future is possible with fintech, where a more interconnected world is being created.

Items.Title: 卡路里追踪器 | Calorie Tracker
Items.Description:
对于我的第 3 阶段项目，我决定制作一个卡路里追踪器。 我在这个项目上工作很开心。 对于后端，我喜欢做的一件事是设置路由以查看我的数据库的各种端点。 为了查看完整的数据库，我保持端点简单。 此代码块所做的是获取类 Day 的所有实例并将其转换为 JSON。 我还创建了一个端点以通过 id /days/:id获取单独的一天

get "/days/:id" do day = Day.find(params[:id]) day.to_json end

这是从端点获取 id，并搜索该类的所有实例，直到找到具有匹配 id 的实例。

当数据库增长时，在一个页面上查看的天数太多时，我想将应用程序更改为一次只显示 7 天，并添加前进和后退按钮。 因此，我添加了一个端点来获取最近 7 天的数据。 /day/last_seven

 get "/days/last_seven" do week = Day.last(7) week.to_json end

这是在类中搜索最后七个实例并将它们转换为 JSON。 接下来，我有一个端点，将属于一天的所有食物与每一天嵌套在一起。 /days/with_foods

 get "/days/with_foods" do days = Day.all days.to_json(include: :foods) end

这是抓取该类的所有实例并转换为 JSON，它包括属于那一天的所有食物。 最后，我将最后两个端点结合起来创建一个嵌套食物数据的端点，并且只显示最近 7 天的数据。 /days/last_seven/with_foods

 get "/days/last_seven/with_foods" do week = Day.last(7) week.to_json(include: :foods) end

我省略了我的食物终点，因为它们主要反映了日子终点。 他们只是在与不同的班级互动。 至于我的 POST、PATCH 和 DELETE 端点，食物端点更有趣一些，因为表格有更多列。 所以，我的 DELETE /foods/:id看起来像这样：

 delete '/foods/:id' do food = Food.find(params[:id]) food.destroy end

该路由在 Food 类中查找食物实例并删除该实例。 我的 POST foods 端点如下所示：

 post '/foods' do food = Food.create( name: params[:name], calories: params[:calories], fat: params[:fat], fiber: params[:fiber], day_id: params[:day_id] ) food.to_json end

这是创建一个新实例，参数是在请求正文中从前端发送的。 使用这些参数，我用每个键填充相应的值，然后将实例转换为 JSON。 最后，我的 PATCH 路线如下所示：

 patch '/foods/:id' do food = Food.find(params[:id]) food.update( name: params[:name], calories: params[:calories], fat: params[:fat], fiber: params[:fiber], day_id: params[:day_id] ) food.to_json end

这是从 url 中获取 id，在类中搜索具有匹配 id 的实例，使用从前端请求正文中收到的新数据更新实例，并将其全部转换为 JSON。

我真的很喜欢这个项目，并且考虑了我可能想要访问的所有不同路径以与前端应用程序进行交互。



原文:

For my phase 3 project I decided to make a calorie tracker. I had a lot of fun working on this project. For the backend, one thing I enjoyed working on was setting up the routes to view the various endpoints for my databases. For viewing the full database I kept my endpoint simple. /days and /foods

get "/days" do
  days = Day.all
  days.to_json
end

What this code block is doing is grabbing all instances of the class Day and converting it to JSON. I also created an endpoint to get an individual day by id /days/:id

get "/days/:id" do
  day = Day.find(params[:id])
  day.to_json
end

This is grabbing the id from the endpoint, and searching through all of the instances of the class until it finds the instance with the matching id.

When the database grows, and there gets to be too many days to view on one page, I would like to change the application to only display seven days at a time and add buttons to go forward and back. So, I added an endpoint to grab the most recent seven days. /day/last_seven

get "/days/last_seven" do
  week = Day.last(7)
  week.to_json
end

This is searching for the last seven instances in the class and converting them to JSON. Next, I have an endpoint that nests all foods belonging to a day with each day. /days/with_foods

get "/days/with_foods" do
  days = Day.all
  days.to_json(include: :foods)
end

This is grabbing all instances of the class and converting to JSON and it includes all foods belonging to that day. Finally, I combined the last two endpoints to create an end point that nests food data, and only shows the last seven days. /days/last_seven/with_foods

get "/days/last_seven/with_foods" do
  week = Day.last(7)
  week.to_json(include: :foods)
end

I have left out my foods endpoints because they mostly mirror the days endpoints. They are just interacting with a different class. as for my POST, PATCH, and DELETE endpoints the food endpoints are a little more interesting because the tables have more columns. So, my DELETE /foods/:id looks like this:

delete '/foods/:id' do
  food = Food.find(params[:id])
  food.destroy
end

This route is finding the instance of the food in the Food class and deleting that instance. My POST foods endpoint looks like:

post '/foods' do
  food = Food.create(
    name: params[:name],
    calories: params[:calories],
    fat: params[:fat],
    fiber: params[:fiber],
    day_id: params[:day_id]
  )
  food.to_json
end

This is creating a new instance, and the params are sent from the front end in the body of the request. With those params I fill in the coresponding values with each key and then convert the instance to JSON. And finally, my PATCH route looks like:

patch '/foods/:id' do
  food = Food.find(params[:id])
  food.update(
    name: params[:name],
    calories: params[:calories],
    fat: params[:fat],
    fiber: params[:fiber],
    day_id: params[:day_id]
  )
  food.to_json
end

This is grabbing the id from the url, searching through the class for the instance with the matching id, updating the instance with the new data recieved from the body of the request from the front end, and converting it all to JSON.

I really enjoyed this project and thinking about all of the different routes I may want to access to interact with the front end application.

Items.Title: 成长，进化，发展。 | Growing, Evolving, Developing.
Items.Description:
我从来没有想过大学辍学后，我会发现自己处于一个为期 15 周的软件工程训练营的位置，但令我惊讶的是，事实证明这是一次真正塑造了我的经历我的大部分世界。 从我高中的早期开始，我就有学习如何编码的倾向，直到最近几周我才真正参加。

当我继续通过我的经验来确定我想认真对待的职业道路作为最近的大学辍学时，我非常感谢我对现在即将结束的事情采取了这种信念的飞跃。 有很多事情让我意识到现在是我按照这种倾向采取行动的好时机，但几个月前，我意识到在重组环境中进行教育的想法。

如我所愿，有时我会完成我的学位，我非常理解为什么这种学校对像我这样的人如此有帮助，他们可能并不总是有足够的精力去掌握大量的课程材料他们毕业后可能永远不会碰。 我不知道会发生什么，而且，出于某种原因，我很幸运能在我做出选择的时候做出选择，因为我不仅终于找到了一些我可以轻松地花费所有时间去做的事情，而且

我还发现了一个由个人组成的社区，他们将永远在我心中占据一个非常特殊的位置。 将我们聚集在一起的东西很美好，也许是无聊，或者是对我们所处的地方的不满，以及探索一些可能使我们能够进入可能让我们获得某种自由的职业道路的东西的动力，两者经济上和智力上。

我不能为我的同龄人中的其他人说太多，但我可以肯定地说，我有幸与我们非常有动力的人一起学习软件工程，他们有许多与我相同的抱负。 从我在新兵训练营的时间结束的这个时间点向前迈进，一个可怕的下一步出现在我面前，我不确定我将如何努力。

无论现在和不久的将来科技领域的状况如何，我都知道各种工程将是一项极其宝贵的资产，现在我可以理解我们在一个平台上与多少网站和界面进行交互日常或结构化，我对许多开发人员有更深刻的理解和赞赏，他们努力构建和维护我们互联网世界的基础。

我认为我对我的教育工作感到最有趣的是，也许我的初衷实际上是学习网络安全的想法。 我知道及时我可能会考虑到这一点，因为现在我已经锁定了一些主要的开发基础知识，我应该更容易了解其他代码、基础和语言，但总会有一部分我可能最喜欢软件工程。

现在我不确定这是因为我可以自己构建一个完整的堆栈应用程序并且未来项目的金钱想法总是浮现在我的脑海中，还是因为我在 Boot Camp 的亲身经历让我感到非常安慰时间，我不确定我的下一步行动是什么，考虑到最近几年的情况，我没有看到很多人，也没有很大的灵感来扩展我的 Netwerk 或尝试蹲在空间里我不属于。 关于开发社区，我最明确地理解的一件事是，与兴趣广泛的个人在一起是多么美妙，但仍然有一个共同点，以及不断学习和发展的愿望他们中的每一个。 这可以用于许多不同的环境，正如我相信你可以想象的那样，我认为令人难以置信的是，我们在编码之外拥有的不同技能和知识只会让我们更有想象力地构建。

我在软件工程新手训练营的 15 周可能不会让我在没有任何进一步投资或扩展我的知识的情况下立即获得成功，这无疑让我对冒险的想法大开眼界并冒险进入未知世界，希望它可以促进对自己或更广泛的整体生活背景的进一步了解。 当我第一次开始 Boot Camp 时，我写了一篇关于我通过代码观察生活的博客文章，现在我已经结束了这 15 周，这些理解只会变得更加深刻。

不，我仍然是一个婴儿开发者，还有很长的路要走，我很感激我至少可以理解我的局限性，以及有很大改进空间的领域。 我最大的抱负之一是让发展成为我自己的主要关注点，希望能在某个时间点做我喜欢做的工作，甚至可能在传统工作环境之外做这件事。 在我最初进入 Web3 空间时，我被介绍给许多开发人员，并且发现他们中的许多人都有这种情绪。

虽然我知道我的旅程是我自己的，并且不能保证它会如何成功，但我很高兴采取下一步行动来加深我对刚刚学到的东西的理解，并继续运用学习的技巧，如何学习融入我生活中的每一个其他环境。 我学到的最重要的是，没有一种方法可以解决问题，而对于我们中的许多人来说，在从失败中学习的过程中找到自己的最佳状态可能是到达那里的最佳方式。

总而言之，我对在新兵训练营度过的时光感激不尽，并将永远回顾过去的 15 周，这是迄今为止我 21 年中最具变革性的时期之一，我将进一步承担一切总的来说，随着它继续展开，我已经了解了我生活的更大范围。

感谢您阅读本文，无论您以何种方式发现它，无论它以何种形式可能对您有价值，平安无事我感谢这个由开发人员和出色的个人组成的社区，他们始终如一地不断努力互相帮助了解我们作为开发人员在追求发展的过程中所面临的许多困难。

我希望这在某种程度上激发了你内心的火焰，让你考虑一个新的视角，我感谢你花时间在我的脑海中迈出一步。 如果您有任何问题，或想与我们保持联系，请随时给我发消息，或在下方发表评论。

爱，

NESSA KODO NESSA 工藤



原文:

I never would’ve thought that upon dropping out of college, I would ever find myself in the position of taking up a 15 week software engineering, Boot Camp, but to my surprise, it turned out to be an experience that has truly shaped so much of my world. Since my early high school years, I’ve had this inclination to learn how to code, and I’ve never properly attended to until recently weeks.

As I continue to navigate through my experience of identifying what career path I want to take seriously as a recent college dropout, I am very grateful that I took this leap of faith towards some thing that is now coming to a close. The were many things that brought me to the point of realizing it was fine time for me to act upon this inclination, but a few months ago I was awakened to the idea of education in the a restructured context.

As much as I wish, I would’ve finished out my degree sometimes, I very much understand why the sort of schools can be so helpful for individuals like myself, who may not always have the mental expenditure to grasp a large scope of course materials the likely never touch once they graduate. I had no idea what to expect, and, for some reason was very lucky to have made my choice at the very time that I did, because not only did I finally find some thing I could easily spend all of my hours doing, but

I also found a community of individuals who will always hold a very special place in my heart. there is something beautiful about what brought us all together, perhaps it was boredom, or maybe dissatisfaction with where we were, and a drive to explore some thing that might enable us to enter a career path that might allow us a sort of freedom, both financially, and intellectually.

I can’t speak so much for everyone else in my cohort, but I most definitely can say the individuals I had the pleasure of learning software engineering with our extremely driven people with many of the same ambitions I have. moving forward from this point in time in which my time in Boot Camp is now done, a scary next step is marked in front of me, one which I’m not sure exactly how I will strive towards.

Regardless of the state of the tech space both now, and in the near future, I know that engineering of all kinds will be an extremely valuable asset, and now that I can understand how many of the websites and interfaces we all interact with on a day-to-day basis or structured, I have a much deeper understanding and appreciation for the many developers, who work so hard to build and maintain the fundamentals of our Internet world.

I think what I find most amusing about my undertaking of this schooling is, perhaps the idea that my initial intention was actually to study cyber security. I know in time I will likely come around to this as now that I have some of the primary development fundamentals locked down it should be much easier for me to get to know other code, bases and languages, but there will always be a part of me that might just love software engineering most of all.

Now I’m not sure whether this is because I can build a full stack application myself and the money ideas for future projects are always popping into my head, or because my experience in an in person, Boot Camp was so comforting to me following a time, where I wasn’t sure what my next move was going to be, And given the circumstances of recent years, I didn’t see many people and wasn’t very inspired to expand my Netwerk or to try to crouch in spaces in which I don’t belong. Some thing I have most definitely understood about the development community is how wonderful it is to be in the presence of individuals who have such a wide span of interests, yet still, there is a common thread, and a desire to constantly learn and evolve within every one of them. This could be taken into many different contexts, as I’m sure you could imagine, and I think it’s incredible that the different skill sets and knowledge we all have outside of coding only enable us to build more imaginatively.

The my 15 weeks in a software engineering boot camp likely will not set me up for success right off of the bat without any further investment of my time or expansion of my knowledge, it has most definitely open my eyes up to the idea of taking risks and venturing into the unknown with hopes that it might facilitate some further understanding of oneself or furthermore, the greater context of life overall. I wrote a blog post when I first started Boot Camp about my observation of life through code, and now that I’m at the end of those 15 weeks, those understandings have only become even more realized.

No I am still a baby dev and have quite a long road ahead of me, I’m grateful that I can at least understand my limitations, as well as areas where there lies great room for improvement. One of my biggest ambitions with making development a primary focus for myself, is to hopefully reach a point in time where I can do what I love as a job, as well as maybe even do it outside of a traditional work environment. I was introduced to many developers in my initial on boarding into the Web3 space, and found this sentiment to be shared among many of them.

Though I know my journey is my own, and there’s no guarantee how it will pan out, I am very excited to take the next steps in advancing my understanding of what I have just learned, as well as continuing to take the very skill of learning, how to learn into every other context of my life. what I have learned most of all is that there is no one way to approach a situation to the point of resolve, and for many of us, finding our own groove as we learn through failure might just be the best way to get there anyway.

In synopsis, I couldn’t be more grateful for my time in Boot Camp, and will forever look back at the last 15 weeks I was one of the most transformational periods of my 21 years thus far, and I will further more carry everything that I have learned into the greater scope of my life, overall as it continues to unfold.

Thank you for reading this, in whatever way you found it, and in whatever form it might have been a value to you, peace out I am grateful for this community of developers and wonderful individuals who consistently and continuously make great efforts to help one another understand many of the difficulties we all face in our pursuits to build an evolve as developers.

I hope that in some way, this has sparked a flame within you to consider a new perspective, and I appreciate you taking the time to take a step within my mind. If you have any questions, or would like to be in touch, feel free to message me, or comment below.

Instagram Twitter Discord

Love,

NESSA KODO

Items.Title: #gitPanic - 藏起来 | #gitPanic - Stash
Items.Description:
刚刚意识到你一直在错误的分支机构工作？ 开始工作前忘记拉动？ 把 git stash 想象成把你的工作目录塞进你的口袋里以备后用。

本博客假设您对 git 有基本的了解，或者已经阅读了Git 101和Merging and Rebasing 。

  1. 工作目录
  2. 藏匿处
  3. 使用你的藏品
  4. 部分藏品

工作目录

当您进行更改时，它会记录差异或差异。 工作目录就像是所有尚未保存的工作的状态变量。

您已经在文本编辑器中打开了您的存储库。 你做出改变。 您保存文​​件。 除非文件在你的.gitignore中，否则 git 会跟踪更改。 跟踪的更改作为未暂存的更改添加到您的工作目录。

只要你使用git add . 它们是您工作目录中的暂存更改。

一旦您提交了更改，它们就被有效地保存了。 这就是为什么将未暂存的更改与文件的旧版本进行比较称为工作树。

此时您可能会想“提交阶段性更改是将内容保存在我的工作目录中的唯一方法”，而您错了。

藏匿处

我喜欢将 stash 看作是我的 git 树底部的一个桶，为我保存我的工作目录的快照。

从技术上讲，跑步

在你的分支之外的一些提交中记录你当前的工作目录，并再次为你检查HEAD 。 与 git 中的大多数可读内容一样，stash 是对这些提交的引用，并且你的 repo 中的所有 stashes 都存储在.git/refs/stash中。 这意味着无论您签出哪个分支，您都可以访问您曾经藏匿的所有内容。

您可以存储已暂存和未暂存的文件。 如果您创建一个新文件，则必须运行git add <file path>以将其包含在存储中。 如果只想存储暂存文件，可以传递-S 。 如果您传递以--开头的文件路径，则只会隐藏该文件或目录中的更改。 如果你想保存未跟踪文件的状态，你可以通过-u选项。 如果你想隐藏被忽略的文件，你可以通过-a 。

普通的旧git stash会自动为您创建一条消息，例如“保存的工作目录和索引状态 WIP on <branch-name> : <last commit SHA and message subject> ”。 请记住，所有藏品都保存在同一个地方。 当您运行git stash list时，它会列出您存储库中的所有存储。 跑

写你自己的信息 - 未来你会感谢你。

使用你的藏品

如果你想再次使用你隐藏的工作目录，你可以运行

或者

要删除存储而不应用它，请运行

如果你想应用、弹出或删除一个不是你最近创建的一个，你传递索引。

要获取索引，请运行

你会看到你的藏品清单。 他们每个人都有stash@{x}和开头，其中x是索引号。 您以相同的方式传递索引，例如

git stash apply stash@ { 1 }

也许您开始进行更改并意识到要保存工作，但将其放在另一个分支中。 要从存储中创建新分支，请运行

git stash branch <branch name> stash@ { x }

部分藏品

事实证明，运行git stash就像用训练轮运行git stash push一样。 除了选项或文件路径之外，它不允许您向命令传递任何内容。 换句话说，除了文件路径之外，您不能传递无效或拼写错误的内容。

另一方面， git stash push不会为你检查这些东西，你可以传递任何你想要的东西。 这意味着git stash -p也不会被检查，它是git stash push -p的别名。 运行git stash -p将打开一个交互模式编辑窗口，并带您逐块完成所有更改。 每个大块头都是一个差异。

就像多项选择测验一样，每个大块头都会遇到“藏起这个大块头？”的问题。 你有几个选项可以回答。

  • 您不会看到打印的常用选项使用? 是/和s 。 您可以使用正则表达式搜索所有帅哥/ 。 如果你想将一个大块分成更多的大块，请使用s 。 交互模式补丁 git 参考文档列出了所有选项。

    您可以随时使用ctrl + c停止进程或按q退出。 如果您使用q ，您已经选择隐藏的任何帅哥都将被隐藏。

    结论

    我最喜欢的 git 命令可能是git stash 。 我很高兴了解部分存储 - 我已经可以想到它们会在很多场景中发挥作用。



原文:

Just realized you've been working in the wrong branch? Forgot to pull before you started working? Think of git stash as stuffing your working directory in your pocket for later.

This blog assumes you have a basic understanding of git or have read Git 101 and Merging and Rebasing.

  1. Working Directory
  2. The Stash
  3. Using Your Stashes
  4. Partial Stashes

Working Directory

At its core, git is a data structure for all the changes you make to your code. It makes a record of diffs or differences when you make changes. The working directory is like a state variable for all your work you haven't saved.

You've got your repo open in your text editor. You make a change. You save the file. Unless the file is in your .gitignore, the changes are tracked by git. Tracked changes are added to your working directory as unstaged changes.

As soon as you use git add . they are staged changes in your working directory.

Once you commit the changes, they're effectively saved. Git will add them to the tree it keeps for you repo and remove them from your working directory. This is why comparing the unstaged changes to the old version of the file is called the working tree.

At this point you may be thinking "committing my staged changes is the only way to save stuff in my working directory," and you'd be wrong.

The Stash

I like to think of the stash as a bucket at the base of my git tree holding onto snapshots of my working directory for me.

Technically, running

records your current working directory in some commits outside your branch and checks out HEAD again for you. Like most things readable in git, a stash is a ref to those commits, and all of the stashes in your repo are stored in .git/refs/stash. This means you have access to everything you've ever stashed no matter what branch you have checked out.

You can stash staged and unstaged files. If you create a new file, you have to run git add <file path> to include it in a stash. If you want to stash only staged files, you can pass -S. If you pass a file path starting with --, only changes in that file or directory will be stashed. If you wanted to save the state of untracked files, you can pass the -u option. If you want to stash ignored files, you can pass -a.

Plain old git stash automatically creates a message for you, like "Saved working directory and index state WIP on <branch-name>: <last commit SHA and message subject>". Remember all the stashes are saved in the same place. When you run git stash list, it lists all of the stashes in your repo. Run

to write your own message - future you will thank you.

Using Your Stashes

If you want to use the working directory you stashed again, you can run

or

Apply will apply the last stash you made. Pop will apply the last stash you made and delete the stash.

To delete a stash without applying it, run

If you want to apply, pop, or drop one that is not the most recent one you made, you pass the index.

To get the index, run

and you'll see a list of your stashes. Each of them will have stash@{x} and the beginning, where x is the index number. You pass the index the same way, like

git stash apply stash@{1}

Maybe you started making changes and realized you wanted to save the work, but put it in another branch. To create a new branch from a stash, run

git stash branch <branch name> stash@{x}

Partial Stashes

Turns out, running git stash is like running git stash push with training wheels. It doesn't allow you to pass anything to the command but options or a file path. In other words, you can't pass something that won't work or is misspelled, except for the file path.

On the other hand, git stash push doesn't check these things for you, and you can pass whatever you want. This means git stash -p, which is an alias for git stash push -p will also not be checked. The -p or --partial option creates a partial stash.

Running git stash -p will open an interactive mode editing window and take you through all your changes hunk by hunk. Each hunk is a diff.

Like a multiple choice quiz, every hunk is presented with the question "Stash this hunk?" and you have several options for your answer.

  • y = yes, stash that hunk
  • n = no, don't stash that hunk
  • a = stash this hunk, and all the hunks in this file
  • d = do not stash this hunk, or any hunks in this file
  • e = manually edit the current hunk
  • ? = help, explain my options to me

Commonly used options that you won't see printed using ? are / and s. You can search all the hunks with regex with /. If you want to split a hunk into more hunks, use s. The interactive mode patch git reference documentation lists all your options.

You can stop the process with ctrl + c or hit q to quit at anytime. If you use q, any hunks you've already chosen to stash will be stashed.

Conclusion

My favorite git command just might be git stash. I was excited to learn about partial stashes - I can already think of quite a few scenarios in which they'll be useful.

Sometimes we want to share to our users what’s going on with his network requests, because as we all know, unfortunately those the network requests can fail, and when they do, handling and displaying them in an automated and reusable way without not repeating ourselves rewriting try/catch blocks all over and then somehow handling those errors and displaying them in the UI.

I will assume that you already have basic knowledge of Redux, ReduxToolkit and React.

Lets start creating a simple create-react-app with a reducer that will set the reusable modals whenever the network requests

First, we'll create actions using the createAsyncThunk method, whether they are actually async or not. You can still use them to dispatch data to the reducers as if the were in the reducers object of the slice .

Here's the js version to make it more clear Next, we’ll create two thunks, one to get a positive response and one to force an error. Now let’s add a button in the component that will trigger these two requests. Our component — I did this project with create-react-app— will look like this:

Items.Title: Nuxt 3 中的事件总线模式具有完整的 TypeScript 支持 | Event Bus Pattern in Nuxt 3 with full TypeScript support
Items.Description:
我发现需要在一些常见的用户交互中运行某些操作。 这些交互也可以是相同的，但在应用程序的完全不同的部分，因此需要可重用性。 我发现使用事件总线模式来以结构化和有序的方式来通知这些正在发生的交互并对它们做出反应的最简单方法是使用事件总线模式。

使用事件总线是一种简单的方式来交流我们应用程序的不同部分。 事件可以从我们应用程序的任何地方发出（有或没有有效负载），然后触发一些可能挂接到此特定事件的侦听器函数（它将接收有效负载作为参数）。 就像 Vue 组件发出一个事件，然后它的父组件可以捕获并运行一些代码作为响应。 事件总线本质上是相同的，但在没有父子结构的更全局范围内。

自 Vue 3 发布以来，使用 Vue 实例作为事件总线不再是可行的方法。 如果我们希望在我们的应用程序中实现这种模式，我们可能需要一个像Mitt这样的库，它是vuejs.org本身推荐的（我在文档中找不到它了，但我很确定它在那里）。

在本文中，我想向您展示我是如何使用Mitt在我的 Nuxt 3 应用程序中实现这种模式的，它已经提供了完整的 TypeScript 支持。 为了完全理解幕后发生的事情，我建议在继续阅读之前快速阅读Mitt 文档。

让我们创建一个插件 ( plugins/event-bus.ts ) 并基本上将我们需要的功能从 Mitt 映射到我们的应用程序。

我假设您对 Nuxt 插件和助手提供程序的工作方式有一些了解。 访问 自动提供助手了解更多信息。

我已将这些方法映射到event和listen (Laravel) 以避免潜在的冲突和混淆。

 import mitt from ' mitt ' export default defineNuxtPlugin (() => { const emitter = mitt () return { provide : { event : emitter . emit , // Will emit an event listen : emitter . on // Will register a listener for an event } } })

这将使我们仅通过使用useNuxtApp可组合项就可以在应用程序的任何位置使用$event和$listen函数：

 // components/register.vue const { $event } = useNuxtApp () const onUserRegistered = ( user ) => { // User registration form was saved and then we want // to notify the entire application about that. $event ( ' user:registered ' , user ) }

然后我们可以在任何我们想要的地方捕获这个事件：

 // app.vue // ideally you will have a plugin (or multiple) to register listeners const { $event } = useNuxtApp () $listen ( ' user:registered ' , ( user ) => { console . log ( ' A user was registered! ' , user ) // do something here... // An interesting example for this use case could be to // just register an event or a conversion in your // favourite analytics tool like Google Analytics, Fathom... })

您可以在应用程序的任何位置为同一事件注册多个侦听器。 请注意，在某些用例中可以而且必须删除侦听器。 只是想让这个示例尽可能简单，以参考Mitt 文档以获取更多信息。

这就是事件总线应该如何工作的全部内容！ 但现在我们想更进一步，因为 Mitt 支持 TypeScript，我们可以确保我们也使用安全的有效负载触发安全的事件名称。 再次基于Mitt 文档，我们可以像这样改进我们的插件：

 import mitt from ' mitt ' interface User { name : string } type ApplicationEvents = { // translates to: // user:registered -> event name // User -> type as payload ' user:registered ' : User ' user:deleted ' : User }; export default defineNuxtPlugin (() => { // Then we just inform mitt about our event types const emitter = mitt < ApplicationEvents > () return { provide : { event : emitter . emit , // Will emit an event listen : emitter . on // Will register a listener for an event } } })

瞧！ 我们的编辑已经意识到这一点，并会相应地提出建议：

但不仅如此，有效载荷也是类型化的，因​​此如果我们没有提供正确的类型，我们的应用程序将发出警告：

现在我们准备好使用我们的事件总线来触发我们喜欢的任何事件并捕获它们进行处理。

结论

在这里，我展示了一个非常基本的示例，但您的应用程序可以触发和响应的事件数量没有限制。

我发现这种模式对于代码的可重用性和使我的应用程序的某些部分可扩展特别有用。 与第三方服务集成（如上面的示例）或显示通知可能是几个例子。 在这有用的地方随意发挥你的想象力。

原文:

I found the need to run certain actions at some common user interactions. These interactions could also be the same but in totally different parts of the application so required reusability. The easiest way I found to have a structured and ordered way to either notify about these interactions happening and react to them was using the Event Bus Pattern.

Using an event bus is an easy way to communicate different parts of our application. Events can be emitted from anywhere in our app (with or without payload) and then trigger some listener functions (which will receive the payload as parameter) that may be hooked to this particular event. Just like a Vue component emits an event, then its parent can capture and run some code in response. The event bus is essentially the same but in a more global scope without the parent-child structure.

Since the release of Vue 3, using a Vue instance as event bus is no longer the way to go. If we are looking to implement this pattern in our app we may require a library like Mitt, which was recommended by vuejs.org itself (I can't find it anymore in the docs but I am pretty sure it was there).

In this article I want to show you how I have implemented this pattern in my Nuxt 3 applications using Mitt which already provides full TypeScript support. To fully understand what's happening under the hood I recommend to have a quick read of Mitt documentation before continue reading.

Mitt will act as the bus itself so all we have to do is just to create a plugin to be able to use its functionality in a more "Nuxt way". Let's create a plugin (plugins/event-bus.ts) and basically map the functions we need from Mitt to our application.

I am assuming you have some knowledge about how Nuxt plugins and helpers providers work. Visit Automatically Providing Helpers for more info.

Mitt's API and Vue events are quite similar in naming ($emit/emit and $on/on). I have mapped these methods to event and listen (Laravel) to avoid potential collision and confusion.

import mitt from 'mitt' export default defineNuxtPlugin(() => { const emitter = mitt() return { provide: { event: emitter.emit, // Will emit an event listen: emitter.on // Will register a listener for an event } }
})

This will let us use $event and $listen functions anywhere in our application by just using the useNuxtApp composable:

// components/register.vue
const { $event } = useNuxtApp() const onUserRegistered = (user) => { // User registration form was saved and then we want // to notify the entire application about that. $event('user:registered', user)
}

Then we can capture this event wherever we want:

// app.vue 
// ideally you will have a plugin (or multiple) to register listeners const { $event } = useNuxtApp() $listen('user:registered', (user) => { console.log('A user was registered!', user) // do something here... // An interesting example for this use case could be to  // just register an event or a conversion in your  // favourite analytics tool like Google Analytics, Fathom...
})

You can register multiple listeners for the same event anywhere in your application. Note that listeners can and must be removed at certain use cases. Just want to keep this example as simple as possible to refer to Mitt docs for more info.

That's all about how the event bus is supposed to work! But now we want to move one step further and since Mitt supports TypeScript, we can make sure we are firing safe event names with a safe payload too. Based on Mitt docs again we could improve our plugin like so:

import mitt from 'mitt' interface User { name: string
} type ApplicationEvents = { // translates to:  // user:registered -> event name // User -> type as payload 'user:registered': User 'user:deleted': User
}; export default defineNuxtPlugin(() => { // Then we just inform mitt about our event types const emitter = mitt<ApplicationEvents>() return { provide: { event: emitter.emit, // Will emit an event listen: emitter.on // Will register a listener for an event } }
})

Voilà! Our editor is already aware about this and will suggest accordingly:

But not only that, the payload is also typed so our application will warn if we aren't providing the right type:

Now we are ready to use our Event Bus to fire any event we like and capture them for processing.

Conclusion

Here I have shown a very basic example but there is no limit on the amount of events your application can fire and react to.

I find this pattern specially useful for code reusability and making certain parts of my applications extensible. Integrating with third party services (like the example above) or displaying notifications could be a couple of examples. Feel free to use your imagination where this can be useful.
